'''Mike A. Ford''' (born 18 November 1965) is an English [[rugby league]] and [[rugby union]] [[Coach (sport)|coach]], and former rugby league footballer.<ref name="Statistics at rugbyleagueproject.org"/> He was most recently the managing director of [[RFL Championship]] side [[Oldham R.L.F.C.|Oldham]].

==Background==
Ford was born in [[Oldham]], [[Lancashire]], England. He was a pupil at [[Saddleworth School]] in [[Uppermill]], [[Greater Manchester]], from 1976 to 1981, where he was taught and coached by [[Phil Larder]]. Larder later recommended Ford to Ireland when Larder was defence coach of England.

Three of his sons play rugby union. [[George Ford (rugby union)|George]] plays for [[Sale Sharks]], and England. [[Joe Ford (rugby union)|Joe]] is head coach of [[Doncaster Knights]] and his youngest son Jacob was the attack coach for Loughborough Students before being named as head coach for Westcliff Rugby Club In August 2019.

==Playing career==
Ford played [[rugby league]] as a {{rlp|sh}}, playing for [[Wigan Warriors|Wigan]], [[Leigh Centurions|Leigh]], [[Oldham R.L.F.C.|Oldham]] (two spells), [[Sydney Roosters]], [[Castleford Tigers|Castleford]] (two spells), [[South Queensland Crushers]], [[Warrington Wolves|Warrington]], [[Wakefield Trinity]] and [[Bramley RLFC|Bramley]]. He won 10 caps for Great Britain.

Ford played {{rlp|sh}} in [[Wigan Warriors|Wigan]]'s 14–8 victory over [[New Zealand national rugby league team|New Zealand]] in the tour match on Sunday 6 October 1985.<ref name="1985 Tour Match: Wigan 14 New Zealand 8">{{cite web|url=http://wigan.rlfans.com/fusion_pages/index.php?page_id=722|title=1985 Tour Match: Wigan 14 New Zealand 8|publisher=wigan.rlfans.com|date=31 December 2011|access-date=1 January 2012|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131012082526/http://wigan.rlfans.com/fusion_pages/index.php?page_id=722|archive-date=12 October 2013|url-status=dead}}</ref>

Ford played in [[Castleford Tigers|Castleford]]'s 12–28 defeat by Wigan in the [[1992 Challenge Cup|1992]] Challenge Cup Final.

Ford is a [[Castleford Tigers]] Hall Of Fame inductee.<ref name="Hall of Fame at castigers.com">{{cite web|url=http://castigers.com/cgi-bin/halloffame.cgi |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20060506212435/http://www.castigers.com/cgi-bin/halloffame.cgi |url-status=dead |archive-date=6 May 2006 |title=Hall of Fame at castigers.com |publisher=castigers |date=31 December 2008 |access-date=1 January 2009}}</ref>

Ford moved to South Queensland Crushers but played just 15 games there.

Ford made his début for [[Warrington Wolves|Warrington]] on Wednesday 1 November 1995, and he played his last match for Warrington on Monday 8 April 1996, he made his début for [[Wakefield Trinity]] during the 1996 season, and he played his last match for Wakefield Trinity during the 1997 season.

==Coaching career==
===Bramley (rugby league)===
In 1999, Ford joined [[Bramley RLFC|Bramley]] as a [[player-coach]],<ref>{{cite web|last1=Hadfield|first1=Dave|title=Rugby League: Broncos install Stains as coach|url=https://www.independent.co.uk/sport/rugby-league-broncos-install-stains-as-coach-1182721.html |archive-url=https://ghostarchive.org/archive/20220514/https://www.independent.co.uk/sport/rugby-league-broncos-install-stains-as-coach-1182721.html |archive-date=14 May 2022 |url-access=subscription |url-status=live|website=The Independent|access-date=31 July 2014|date=4 November 1998}}</ref> and joined Oldham in a similar role a year later. He retired from playing in 2001 after guiding Oldham to the championship Grand Final. Whilst at Oldham, he started coaching rugby union at [[Dukinfield]] RUFC for 5 seasons, winning a cup and two promotions.

===Ireland (rugby union)===
Ford left Oldham to take over as Defensive Co-ordinator of [[Ireland national rugby union team|Ireland]] in January 2002 and stayed for 4 seasons, winning a triple crown and helping guide Ireland to 3rd in the world rankings. In September 2004, he started working as a defence and skills coach at [[Saracens F.C.|Saracens]], before taking over as head coach in August 2005.

===British & Irish Lions (rugby union)===
He served as a defence coach for the [[British & Irish Lions]] midweek team on their [[2005 British & Irish Lions tour to New Zealand|2005 tour to New Zealand]], remaining undefeated in 7 games (the test side lost the series 3–0). He left his position with Ireland in September 2005.

===England (rugby union)===
In May 2006, Ford became defence coach of [[England national rugby union team|England]], part of the coaching team that guided England to the 2007 World Cup Final.<ref name="Telegraph">{{cite web|url=https://www.telegraph.co.uk/sport/rugbyunion/international/england/2324177/Crisis-meetings-saved-Englands-World-Cup.html|archive-url=https://archive.today/20130505141700/http://www.telegraph.co.uk/sport/rugbyunion/international/england/2324177/Crisis-meetings-saved-Englands-World-Cup.html|url-status=dead|archive-date=5 May 2013|title=The Telegraph, 26 Oct 2007|publisher=Telegraph|date=26 October 2007|access-date=1 January 2012}}</ref> With Ford as defence coach England conceded the fewest points in the 2009 Six Nations (70) and the fewest tries in the 2010 Six Nations (5), and the fewest tries in the pool stages of the 2011 World Cup.

===Bath (rugby union)===
Deciding not to seek a renewal of his England contract,<ref name="BBC">{{cite web|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-union/16291369|title=BBC Sport, 21 Dec 2011|publisher=BBC|date=21 December 2011|access-date=1 January 2012}}</ref> Ford joined the coaching staff at [[Bath Rugby]]. He became head coach at Bath in May 2013 and guided them to a Champions Cup spot and an Amlin Challenge Cup final. In 2014/15 Bath finished 2nd in the Premiership and lost in the Premiership Final to Saracens. Ford was awarded the Aviva Premiership Director of Rugby of the Year award in 2015 while his son George won Player of the Year.<ref>{{cite web|title=Aviva Premiership Rugby Awards|url=http://www.gettyimages.com/event/aviva-premiership-rugby-awards-552998635?#mike-ford-of-bath-poses-with-the-guinness-director-of-rugby-of-the-picture-id474141114|website=Getty Images|access-date=1 September 2016}}</ref> He left Bath in May 2016 after they had finished 9th in the league.<ref>{{cite web|title=Mike Ford: Bath part company with head coach|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-union/36302585|website=BBC Sport|date=16 May 2016 |access-date=16 May 2016}}</ref>

===Toulon (rugby union)===
Ford joined Toulon as head coach in October 2016<ref name="Mike Ford: Ex-Bath head coach named Toulon boss">{{cite web|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-union/37752700|title=Mike Ford: Ex-Bath head coach named Toulon boss|date=2016-10-24|newspaper=BBC Sport|language=en-GB|access-date=2016-12-20}}</ref> but left at the end of the 2016/17 season.<ref>{{cite web|title=Ford to Leave Toulon|url=http://www.skysports.com/rugby-union/news/12321/10809006/toulon-head-coach-mike-ford-to-leave-french-club-at-end-of-season|website=Sky Sports|access-date=3 June 2017}}</ref> He then announced his move to the [[Griffins Rugby|Dallas Griffins]]. Shortly after he joined [[Leicester Tigers]] where he remained until the conclusion of the [[2020–21 Premiership Rugby|2020–21 season]].<ref>{{Cite web|title=Mike Ford to leave Leicester Tigers|url=https://www.leicestertigers.com/news/coaching-team-update-210617-mikeford|access-date=2021-06-17|website=Leicester Tigers|language=en}}</ref>

===Belgium (rugby union)===
In 2022, he coached the [[Belgium national rugby union team]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite news |last=Heppenstall |first=Ross |date=2023 |title=Mike Ford |url=https://www.therugbyjournal.com/rugby-blog/502x5dgvz5cob1qsuxwmmwlmccyykb |access-date=12 September 2025 |work=[[The Rugby Journal]] |volume=23}}</ref>

===Oldham (rugby league)===
On 12 November 2025 he took on the role of head-coach for [[Oldham RLFC]] in the [[RFL Championship]] following the exit of [[Sean Long]]<ref>{{cite web|website=Total RL|date=12 Nov 2025|author=Callum Walker|title=Mike Ford set to take over as head coach of Oldham following Sean Long exit|url=https://www.totalrl.com/mike-ford-set-to-take-over-as-head-coach-of-oldham-following-sean-long-exit/}}</ref>

After Oldham's defeat to [[London Broncos]] on 1 February 2026, Ford announced his immediate departure from the club stating "it has become clear that the direction of travel of the club is not something that I feel I can be a part of."<ref>{{cite news |last1=Coombe |first1=Louis Chapman |title=Mike Ford announces shock immediate Oldham departure |url=https://www.loverugbyleague.com/post/mike-ford-announces-shock-oldham-departure-with-immediate-effect |access-date=1 February 2026 |work=Love Rugby League |date=1 February 2026 |language=en}}</ref>

== Executive career ==
In 2023, Ford led a consortium that purchased his hometown team, [[Oldham R.L.F.C.]], and acted as interim head coach until the end of that season.<ref>{{Cite news |date=24 March 2023 |title=Oldham announce Mike Ford-led consortium takeover |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/65063886 |access-date=12 September 2025 |work=[[BBC Sport Online]]}}</ref><ref name=":0" /> From September 2025 to February 2026 he was the club's Managing Director.<ref>{{Cite news |date=3 September 2025 |title=Oldham MD Mike Ford joins the League Express Podcast |url=https://www.totalrl.com/oldham-md-mike-ford-joins-the-league-express-podcast/ |access-date=12 September 2025 |work=Total RL (the website of [[League Express]])}}</ref>
